text: We can see this in the following graph. 7.1.9 a) Since the dog and the duck are moving at the same speed, they will both move the same amount in the time it takes θ to change by Δ θ . Thus the problem can be done using Δ θ instead of Δ t in the derivation. During a small amount of time Δ t , the length of the segment R decreases from the dog swimming a length of Δ θ and increases from the duck moving along the perimeter of the circle also by a length of Δ θ . We’ll eventually be taking a limit, so we can make our lives easier with some approximations. The line segment along the perimeter of the circle is approximately the length of the arc that the line segment spans, and the angle bordering ϕ is approximately a right angle. Using these approximations, the law of cosines, and a trick for taking a limit, we can arrive at a differential equation for R . Δ R ≈   − Δ θ   +   c   −   R =   − Δ θ   +   ( Δ θ ) 2   +   R 2   −   2 Δ θ R   cos ( ϕ   +   π 2 )   −   R =   − Δ θ   +   ( Δ θ ) 2   +   R 2   −   2 Δ θ R   cos ( ϕ   +   π 2 )   −   R 2 ( Δ θ ) 2   +   R 2   −   2 Δ θ R   cos ( ϕ   +   π 2 )   +   R =   − Δ θ   +   ( Δ θ ) 2   +   2 Δ θ R   sin ( ϕ ) ( Δ θ ) 2   +   R 2   +   2 Δ θ R   sin ( ϕ )   +   R Δ R Δ θ   =   − 1   +   Δ θ   +   2 R   sin ( ϕ ) ( Δ θ ) 2   +   R 2   +   2 Δ θ R   sin ( ϕ )   +   R lim Δ θ → 0   Δ R Δ θ =   − 1   +   Δ θ   +   2 R   sin ( ϕ ) ( Δ θ ) 2   +   R 2   +   2 Δ θ R   sin ( ϕ )   +   R R ′ =   − 1   +   2 R   sin ( ϕ ) R 2   +   R   =   − 1   +   sin ( ϕ ) For the ϕ ’ equation we can use some basic geometry. Next we have to remember that Δ acb is similar to Δ dce (angles subtended by the same arc are equal), meaning the ratio between a b ¯ and d e ¯ is equal to the ratio of a c ¯ and c e ¯ . Approximating a b ¯ as a b ⌢   =   Δ θ and d e ¯ as d e ⌢ a b ¯   :   d e ¯   =   a c ¯   :   c d ¯   ⇒   d e ⌢   ≈   2   cos ( ϕ )   −   R R   Δ θ Next we have to remember that the arc made by the inscribed angle ϕ is half of the arc of the central angle (inscribed angle theorem). Now putting it all together, the change in the central angle 2Δ ϕ is from an increase of arc d e ⌢ and a decrease of arc Δ θ . 2 Δ ϕ =   d e ⌢   −   Δ θ   ≈   2   cos ( ϕ )   −   R R   Δ θ   −   Δ θ   =   2   cos ( ϕ )   −   2 R R   Δ θ R Δ ϕ Δ θ ≈   cos ( ϕ )   −   R lim Δ θ → 0   R Δ ϕ Δ θ =   R ϕ ′   =   cos ( ϕ )   −   R As for whether or not the dog catches the duck, the answer is no. The proof is difficult enough that it is the subject of a published paper. Please take a look at Applications of Center Manifolds by Keith Promislow if you're interested. b) Repeating the derivation now with the speed of the dog as k Δ θ R ′ =   − k   +   sin ( ϕ ) R ϕ ′ =   cos ( ϕ )   −   R c) Assuming there is a limit cycle R ′ =   − k   +   sin ( ϕ )   =   0   ⇒   ϕ   =   arcsin ( k ) R ϕ ′ =   cos ( ϕ )   −   R   =   0   ⇒   R   =   cos ( ϕ )   =   1   −   k 2 So for k   =   1 2 the dog will endlessly chase the duck while asymptotically approaching a circle of radius 3 2 as a limit cycle. 7.2 Ruling Out Closed Orbits 7.2.1 V   =   x 2   +   y 2 7.2.3 V   =   e x   sin ( y ) 7.2.5 Let ẋ = f ( x, y ) and ẏ = g ( x, y ) be a smooth vector field defined on the phase
